title: Big fine for big four bank
full_text:
One of Australia’s big four banks has been hit with a $26m fine after a court ruled it had failed to respond to customers facing financial hardship.
Westpac has been ordered to pay the eight-figure civil penalty by the Federal Court over more than 200 online hardship requests between 2017 and 2023.
In his ruling, Justice Timothy McEvoy said the bank was “grossly negligent” when dealing with the requests.
The scandal centres around incidents involving Westpac and its subsidiaries St George Bank, BankSA and Bank of Melbourne.
The requests came from more than 200 customers who had told their banks they were experiencing financial hardship and struggling to make repayments on home loans, credit cards, personal loans and car loans.
ASIC deputy chair Sarah Court said the bank’s “systemic failures” allowed these customers to “slip through the cracks”.
“Westpac failed the very customers who needed help when they needed it most,” Ms Court said.
“These were customers who were asking for some breathing room for a range of reasons including domestic abuse, natural disasters, serious illness or the loss of their job.” A Westpac spokesman said the bank apologised to affected customers and was “deeply sorry we let them down”.
“We self-reported these issues in 2022 and 2023 and to put things right, we’ve completed a remediation program including refunds of fees and charges, debt waivers and payments for non-financial loss,” the spokesman said.
“We have strengthened our processes and upgraded our online hardship systems to meet the standards our customers deserve.
“Over the period in question Westpac received about 695,000 requests for hardship assistance.
The banking giant referred itself to regulators over flaws found in its system. In his reasons, Justice McEvoy said the contraventions were “very serious”. “While I accept that Westpac’s conduct was not intentional in the sense that it set out deliberately to engage in the contravening conduct or was reckless, I accept ASIC’s submission that Westpac’s conduct was grossly negligent,” Justice McEvoy said.
“The agreed facts and the evidence demonstrates that it was, or ought to have been, clear to Westpac that the systems and processes employed by it in relation to online hardship notices were not fit for purpose.” The documents reveal that while the incidents date back to between 2017 and 2023, senior management were unaware of the issues until February 2022.
